answers.value: Retail sales floor, front aisle near seasonal display, Thompson Family Hardware LLC, 4127 Maple Ridge Avenue, Portland, OR 97214
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: State Exactly How The Loss Damage Occurred
answers.value:
At approximately 2:35 PM on 2026-01-15, a customer, later identified as Ms. Laura Jenkins, was walking down the main front aisle of our hardware store, Thompson Family Hardware LLC, heading toward the paint section. Earlier that afternoon, at roughly 1:50 PM, one of our employees (Evan Miller, sales associate) had assisted another customer with a purchase of two 1-gallon containers of interior latex paint. During that transaction, a small amount of paint from one of the cans appears to have dripped onto the polished concrete floor near the seasonal display area.

The paint drip was not immediately noticed. Our usual procedure is to walk the aisles every 30–45 minutes to look for hazards, but because we were short-staffed that day and there was a rush of customers due to a mid-January sale, the routine floor check in that area was delayed. As a result, the small spill—approximately the size of a dinner plate, slightly smeared from cart traffic—remained on the floor for about 40–45 minutes.

At 2:35 PM, Ms. Jenkins entered the aisle from the front entrance side, pushing a small hand basket cart. The lighting in that area is bright but the paint was a light off‑white color, which blended somewhat with the light gray concrete floor. Security camera footage (which we have preserved and can provide upon request) shows that she was walking at a normal pace, not running or using her phone at the exact moment of the incident, though she had been intermittently checking a shopping list on her phone a few seconds earlier.

As she stepped into the area where the paint had dripped, her right foot landed directly on the slick spot. Her shoe lost traction, causing her right leg to slide forward while her left leg bent awkwardly behind her. She fell sideways and slightly backward, landing primarily on her left hip and lower back, and then bracing herself with her left hand. Several items in the seasonal display (small boxes of LED string lights) were knocked off the shelf when she attempted to grab the shelving unit to steady herself.

One of our staff members, Maria Lopez (cashier), heard the noise of the fall and Ms. Jenkins calling out and immediately went over to assist, followed within a few seconds by Evan Miller, who had been working near the plumbing section. They helped Ms. Jenkins sit up and then sit on a nearby low stock stool. Ms. Jenkins reported immediate pain in her left hip and lower back and some soreness in her left wrist. She did not appear to lose consciousness, and she was able to speak clearly and describe what had happened. She stated that she had slipped on the paint and pointed to the area on the floor, where the paint smear was clearly visible.

Maria brought over our in-store incident report kit, which includes disposable gloves, paper towels, and a cone sign. Evan placed a yellow "Caution – Wet Floor" cone at the location and then cordoned off the immediate area with a portable barrier while Maria stayed with Ms. Jenkins. I, Daniel Thompson (owner/manager), was in the small office doing inventory checks at the time, and I came out to the sales floor at approximately 2:40 PM after being notified by Maria.

When I arrived, Ms. Jenkins was still seated, visibly shaken and in discomfort but alert and oriented. I asked whether she wanted us to call an ambulance. She initially hesitated, saying she wanted to see if the pain improved after a few minutes, but after attempting to stand up with assistance and experiencing increased pain in her left hip and lower back, she agreed that medical evaluation was appropriate. At approximately 2:45 PM, Maria called 911 to request medical assistance.

While we waited for the paramedics, I took photographs of the area, including the paint spill, the surrounding floor, and the seasonal display that had been disturbed, using the store’s work phone. I also took photographs of Ms. Jenkins’ position on the floor as best as was appropriate and respectful, showing the approximate location of her fall. I collected statements from both Maria Lopez and Evan Miller about what they saw and what they did immediately afterward. I also noted that the paint color and brand matched one of the cans that had been sold earlier that day, and I pulled the relevant transaction from our point-of-sale system to confirm the timing for our records.

Portland Fire & Rescue paramedics arrived at approximately 2:57 PM. They assessed Ms. Jenkins on the scene, checking her hip, lower back, and wrist. They recommended that she be transported to the hospital for further evaluation and possible imaging. Ms. Jenkins agreed and was transported by ambulance to Providence Portland Medical Center. Before she left, I obtained her full name, contact information, and a brief statement from her that she had slipped on paint on the floor in our aisle.

Immediately after the incident, once the paramedics and Ms. Jenkins had left, Evan thoroughly cleaned the paint spill using appropriate cleaning supplies and verified that the entire area was free of residue and properly dried. I then reviewed our in-store safety procedures with the staff on duty, emphasizing the importance of timely aisle inspections and immediate cleanup or barricading of any spills, no matter how small. I also pulled the relevant footage from our security cameras and saved it to an external drive for potential review by the insurer or authorities.

In summary, the loss/damage occurred when a customer slipped on a small, unobserved paint spill on our sales floor, resulting in her fall, reported injuries to her hip, back, and wrist, and disturbance of a small portion of our seasonal merchandise display.

answers.value:
The primary property damage was to a small portion of our seasonal merchandise display and minimal soiling of the sales floor.

1) Seasonal merchandise: During the fall, Ms. Jenkins reached toward the seasonal display shelf to steady herself, causing several boxed items to fall to the floor. Specifically:
   - 6 boxes of "EverBright" LED indoor string lights (SKU EB-LD16-WH), retail price $14.99 each. Of these, 3 boxes sustained crushed corners and torn packaging, making them unsuitable for sale at full price. We have moved these to a damaged/discount bin and will likely have to mark them down by 50–75% or discard them entirely depending on functionality. The remaining 3 boxes had only minor cosmetic wear and may still be sold.
   - 2 small decorative ceramic lanterns (SKU CL-OR12), retail price $19.99 each. One lantern was chipped along the top rim and has been deemed unsellable for regular retail; we may attempt to sell it at a steep discount if it is safe to use. The other lantern sustained only minor scuffing that can be cleaned.

2) Store fixtures and flooring:
   - The metal shelving unit itself was not structurally damaged. We inspected the brackets and uprights and found no bending or warping. A small plastic price-tag holder was dislodged and cracked but has been replaced from spare parts on hand (approximate replacement cost under $5).
   - The polished concrete floor where the paint spill occurred was temporarily stained by the off-white latex paint. After the incident, we used an appropriate paint remover and neutral cleaner to remove the paint. There is no apparent permanent discoloration or etching. At this time, we do not anticipate needing any professional floor repair or resurfacing; however, we have taken photographs before and after cleaning in case any latent issues (such as dulling of the finish) appear later.

Overall, the property damage is relatively minor and primarily consists of a small quantity of seasonal inventory that will need to be written off or heavily discounted, plus negligible damage to store fixtures. The more significant concern arising from this incident is the bodily injury to the customer rather than the property damage.

answers.value:
Based on Ms. Jenkins’ statements at the scene and a follow-up phone conversation on 2026-01-18, as well as what she has relayed from her initial medical evaluation, the injuries are as follows:

1) Left hip and lower back injury:
   - Immediately after the fall, Ms. Jenkins reported sharp pain localized to her left hip area, radiating into her lower back. She stated that the pain was around 7 out of 10 when attempting to stand and 5 out of 10 while seated.
   - Paramedics on scene performed a basic assessment, noting tenderness on palpation over the left lateral hip and lumbar region, but no obvious deformity. They immobilized her as a precaution and recommended hospital evaluation.
   - According to Ms. Jenkins’ later report to us, at Providence Portland Medical Center she underwent X‑rays of the pelvis and lumbar spine. The X‑rays reportedly did not show any fractures but indicated soft tissue strain and significant bruising (contusions). She was diagnosed with a left hip contusion and lumbar strain. She was prescribed pain medication and advised to rest, use ice initially and then heat, and follow up with her primary care physician or a physical therapist if symptoms persisted.
   - Over the next few days (as of her 2026-01-18 update to us), she reported difficulty sitting or standing for longer periods, needing assistance with some household tasks, and being unable to drive comfortably for more than short distances due to pain when pressing the pedals and twisting her torso.

2) Left wrist and hand injury:
   - When she fell, Ms. Jenkins instinctively put out her left hand to brace her impact with the floor. At the scene, she reported a dull, throbbing pain in her left wrist and palm, with some mild swelling beginning to appear within 10–15 minutes of the incident.
   - Paramedics wrapped her wrist with a soft support bandage as a precaution.
   - At the hospital, she was given an X‑ray of the wrist, which, according to her, did not show any fractures. She was diagnosed with a left wrist sprain and was advised to wear a wrist brace for support, apply ice, and avoid heavy lifting or repetitive motions with that hand for at least 1–2 weeks.
   - She mentioned some difficulty performing routine tasks such as typing at her job, opening jars, and lifting heavier grocery bags. She also reported that her employer temporarily modified her duties to reduce keyboard and lifting demands.

3) General bruising and soreness:
   - Ms. Jenkins reported visible bruising along her left hip, side, and lower back that became more pronounced over the 24–48 hours following the fall.
   - She described overall stiffness and soreness, particularly in the mornings and after sitting or lying down for extended periods.

4) Emotional distress and inconvenience (as described by Ms. Jenkins):
   - During our follow-up call, she mentioned feeling anxious about walking in stores with polished concrete floors and being more cautious (and somewhat fearful) about potential slips and falls in general.
   - She also noted the inconvenience of needing assistance from her spouse for certain daily activities (carrying laundry baskets, grocery shopping, etc.) and missing at least one partial day of work for medical appointments.

We do not have direct access to her full medical records, so the above is based on her own description to us and what the paramedics relayed at the time of the incident. She has indicated that she may pursue further evaluation or physical therapy if her back and hip pain do not significantly improve over the next few weeks.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: Ms. Jenkins is not our employee, tenant, or a relative of any of our staff or owners to the best of our knowledge. She is a regular retail customer who has shopped at our store several times per year for various home improvement projects. Our relationship with her is solely that of business owner to customer (member of the general public).

answers.value:
On 2026-01-20, we received a written letter and email from Ms. Jenkins’ attorney, Mark D. Holloway of Holloway & Pierce Injury Law, indicating that Ms. Jenkins intends to pursue a claim for bodily injury and associated damages arising from the slip-and-fall incident at our premises on 2026-01-15.

In the correspondence, Attorney Holloway states that they allege negligence on the part of Thompson Family Hardware LLC for failing to maintain the sales floor in a reasonably safe condition, specifically citing the presence of the paint spill and the delay in discovering and cleaning it. The letter asserts that Ms. Jenkins has incurred medical expenses (including emergency room evaluation, imaging, and follow-up visits), lost wages due to missed work, and ongoing pain and suffering related to her hip, back, and wrist injuries.

The letter does not specify an exact dollar amount of damages at this stage but requests that our insurance carrier be notified immediately and that all further communication regarding the incident be directed through his office. It also requests preservation of all relevant evidence, including surveillance footage, incident reports, employee statements, and any photographs taken of the scene and injuries.

We have preserved all such materials and are submitting this claim form to our insurer in response. Copies of the attorney’s letter (dated 2026-01-19) and the email (sent 2026-01-20 at 9:14 AM) are attached to this claim submission as supporting correspondence.
